title: Tumor bed implant for multimodality treatment of at risk tissue surrounding a resection cavity
abstract: A device having a biocompatible polymer as a body and regularly spaced radiation seeds and regularly spaced magnetic materials disposed of within the body of the biocompatible polymer, or uniformly distributed liquid radiation and magnetic fluid materials within a polymer slab, hollow thick wall polymer shell, or thin wall polymer balloon, as suitable for surgical placement within a resection cavity for treatment of at-risk tissue in the tumor margin with local hyperthermia in combination with radiation and potentially also with chemotherapy and/or immunotherapy that is slowly released from the biocompatible polymer.
claims:
Claims What is claimed is: 1. A biocompatible polymer core brachytherapy device comprising: a biocompatible polymer core and radioactive seeds, wherein said radioactive seeds are regularly spaced on or under the surface of said biocompatible polymer, wherein said device provides a radiation dose delivered to tissue surounding the device when implanted in tumor bed following tumor resection. 2. The device of claim 1 wherein the polymer core is fabricated from a one or more component viscous fluid mixture that gels at about body core temperature to form a soft solid implant in the shape of a tumor resection cavity. 3. The device of claim 1 wherein the biocompatible polymer core is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period. 4. The device of claim 1 wherein the biocompatible polymer core is non-resorbable to maintain equal volume and shape after implantation in the tumor bed. 5. The device of claim 1 wherein the seeds are short half-life radioactive seeds suitable for permanent implantation in the body to deliver a desired therapeutic dose of radiation to tissues close to the seed implant. 6. The device of claim 1, wherein the seeds are embedded at a depth within the range of 1-10 mm below the surface of the polymer. 7. The device of claim 1 wherein the polymer core is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period and a therapeutic drug is incorporated within the polymer for slow release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer core is resorbed. 8. The device of claim 1 wherein the polymer core is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period and an immune stimulating compound is incorporated within the polymer for slow release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer core resorbs. 9. A thermobrachytherapy implant device comprising a biocompatible polymer core, radiation seeds, and magnetic materials, wherein the magnetic materials are distributed under the surface or within said polymer core, and wherein said device provides a combination of both brachytherapy radiation and hyperthermia doses delivered to tissue surounding the device when implanted in tumor bed following tumor resection. 10. The device of claim 9, wherein the magnetic materials are cylindrical seeds or spherical pellets, regularly spaced and interspersed with radiation seeds under the surface of polymer core. 11. The device of claim 9, wherein the polymer core is fabricated from a one or more component viscous fluid mixture that gels at or about body core temperature to form a flexible implant in the shape of a tumor resection cavity. 12. The device of claim 9, wherein the polymer is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period. 13. The device of claim 9, wherein the polymer is non-resorbable to maintain equal volume and shape long after implantation in the tumor bed. 14. The device of claim 9, wherein the radiation seeds are short half-life radioactive seeds suitable for permanent implantation in the body to deliver a desired therapeutic dose of radiation to tissues close to the seed implant in combination with heat from the ferromagnetic materials. 15. The device of claim 9, wherein the magnetic materials have a Curie point transition from magnetic to non-magnetic at a desired implant temperature. 16. The device of claim 9, wherein the desired thermoregulating implant temperature is a temperature in the range of 40-100Â°C. 17. The device of claim 9, wherein the desired thermoregulating implant temperature is about 45- 50Â°C. 18. The device of claim 9, wherein the radiation seeds and interspersed ferromagnetic seeds are embedded at a depth within the range of 1-10 mm below the surface of the polymer. 19. The device of claim 9, wherein the biocompatible polymer core is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period and a therapeutic drug is incorporated within the polymer for slow release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer core is resorbed. 20. The device of claim 9 wherein the biocompatible polymer core is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period and an immune stimulating compound is incorporated within the polymer for slow release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer core resorbs. 21. The device of claim 9, wherein the hyperthermia is generated by application of an external force applied to the device. 22. A multimodal implant device comprising a biocompatible polymer core, a therapeutic agent selected from the group consisting of a chemotherapeutic or immunotherapeutic, or combinations thereof, and magnetic materials, wherein the magnetic materials are embedded within said polymer core, and wherein said device provides a combination of chemotherapy and hyperthermia delivered sequentially or simultaneously to tissue surounding the device when implanted in a tumor bed following tumor resection. 23. The device of claim 22, wherein the magnetic materials are regularly spaced cylindrical or spherical ferromagnetic seeds, at controlled depth under the surface of polymer. 24. The device of claim 23, wherein ferromagnetic seeds are embedded at a depth within the range of 1-10 mm below the surface of the polymer. 25. The device of claim 22, wherein the magnetic materials have a Curie point transition from magnetic to non-magnetic at a desired implant temperature. 26. The device of claim 25, wherein the desired thermoregulating implant temperature is a temperature in the range of 40-100Â°C. 27. The device of claim 26, wherein the desired thermoregulating implant temperature is about 45- 50Â°C. 28. The device of claim 23, wherein the magnetic material is comprised of magnetic nanoparticles that are distributed uniformly within the biocompatible polymer. 29. The device of claim 23, wherein the polymer is fabricated from a one or more component viscous fluid mixture that gels at or about body core temperature to form a flexible implant in the shape of a tumor resection cavity. 30. The device of claim 23, wherein the biocompatible polymer core is resorbable in biologic tissue slowly over an extended time period. 31. The device of claim 23, wherein the biocompatible polymer core degrades and resorbs into surrounding tissue quickly at an elevated temperature between 40-100Â°C. 32. The device of claim 23, wherein the biocompatible polymer core degrades and resorbs into surrounding tissue quickly at an elevated temperature between 45-50Â°C and a therapeutic drug is incorporated within the resorbable polymer for quick release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer core is resorbed. 33. The device of claim 23 wherein the biocompatible polymer core is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period and a therapeutic drug is incorporated within the resorbable polymer for slow release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer is resorbed. 34. The device of Claim 23 wherein the biocompatible polymer core is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period and an immune stimulating agent is incorporated within the polymer for slow release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer is resorbed. 35. A multimodality treatment device comprising a biocompatible elastic or expandable polymer shell having an outer and inner suface and comprising regularly spaced radioactive seeds and magnetic materials embedded adjacent to the inner surface of said expandable polymer shell, and a polymer core within said polymer shell. 36. The device of claim 35, wherein said magnetic material comprises a Curie point transition from magnetic to non-magnetic at a desired implant temperature. 37. The device of claim 35, wherein said magnetic material is selected from the group consisting of: ferromagnetic cylindrical seeds, spherical pellets, particles, nanoparticles, ferrofluid, or combinations thereof. 38. The device of claim 37, wherein said magnetic material comprises a Curie point transition from magnetic to non-magnetic at a desired implant temperature in the range of 40-70Â°C. 39. The device of Claim 38, wherein the desired implant temperature generates a polymer shell implant surface temperature of about 45-50Â°C. 40. The device of claim 35, wherein the polymer shell is resorbable. 41. The device of claim 35, wherien the polymer shell is non-resorbable. 42. The device of claim 35, wherein the biocompatible expandable polymer shell is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period and a therapeutic or immunte stimulating drug is incorporated within the polymer shell for slow release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer is resorbed. 43. The device of claim 35 wherein the biocompatible expandable polymer shell is resorbable in biologic tissue over an extended time period and a therapeutic or immune stimulating drug is incorporated within the core for release into surrounding tumor bed as the polymer is resorbed. 44. The device of claim 35, wherein a material is injected between the inner and outer surface so as to expand the outer surface to fill the resection cavity. 45. A multimodality implant device comprising a biocompatible expandable thin wall polymer shell with a hollow central core, radioactive and magnetic materials, and wherein the polymer shell is capable of expanding to fill a tumor resection cavity. 46. The device of claim 44, wherein a material is injected into the core to expand the outer polymer shell to fill a tumor resection cavity. 47. The device of claim 44, wherein the material contains saline. 48. The device of claim 44, wherein the material comprises a biocompatible resorbable liquid polymer. 49. The device of claim 44, wherein the material comprises a biocompatible non-resorbable liquid polymer. 50. The device of claim 44, wherein the material filling the core comprises a radioactive fluid suitable for permanent implantation in the body (e.g. Iotrex, Cesitrex). 51. The device of claim 44, wherein the material comprises biocompatible uniformly distributed magnetic nanoparticle solution. 52. The device of claim 50, wherein the magnetic nanoparticle solution is iron oxide nanoparticles with dextran. 53. The device of claim 44, wherein the radioactive and magnetic materials are intermixed and distributed uniformly within the core. 54. The device of claim 44, wherein the material comprises a biocompatible magnetic materials such as magnetic powder, small spheres, pellets or seeds. 55. The device of claim 35, wherein the material comprises a biocompatible magnetic material that undergoes Curie Point transition at a temperature within the range of 40-100Â°C. 56. The device of claim 44, wherein the material comprises a biocompatible magnetic material that undergoes Curie Point transition at a temperature of about 45-50Â°C. 57. The device of claim 44, wherein the material comprises a chemotherapeutic agent or liposome encapsulated therapeutic drug. 58. The device of claim 44 wherein the material comprises a immune stimulating agent. 59. The device of claim 44 wherein the material is an expandable material. 60. A multimodality treatment device comprising a biocompatible expandable polymer shell balloon, wherein the polymer shell is capable of expanding to fill a tumor resection cavity, a radioactive material, a magnetic material, a hollow central space containing one or more catheters, and a flexible shaft connecting the balloon implant to the tissue surface. 61. The device of claim 60 wherein at least one catheter extends from outside the skin surface to the tip of polymer shell and allows remote afterloading insertion of a High-Dose-Rate (HDR) brachytherapy source into the central space inside the polymer shell. 62. The device of claim 60 wherein one or more catheters extend from outside the skin surface to various locations inside the hollow space of the polymer shell to allow insertion of temperature monitoring probes. 63. The device of claim 60 wherein one catheter extends from outside the skin surface to the hollow space and suitable to fill the interior space with a material to expand the balloon to fill the resection cavity. 64. The device of claim 60, wherein the hollow space of the expandable polymer shell is filled with magnetic nanoparticle solution and one or more catheters for HDR brachytherapy. 65. A multimodality treatment device comprising a first biocompatible expandable polymer shell balloon, and a second polymer shell; wherein the first and second polymer shells are capable of expanding to fill a tumor resection cavity, a radioactive material, a magnetic material, a hollow central space containing one or more catheters within the second polymer shell, and a void between the first and second polymer shells, and a flexible shaft connecting the balloon implant to the tissue surface. 66. The device of claim 65 wherein a first fluid is injected into the hollow central space and a second fluid is injected into the void between the first and second polymer shells. 67. A method of treating a cancerous tissue in a body comprising placing, adjacent to said tissue, a device according to claims 1-66. 68. A method of treating a canceours tissue in a body comprising inserting into a resection cavity, a device according to claims 1-66. 69. Use of a device according to claims 1-66 for treating cancerous cells in a body adjacent to a resection cavity. 70. A method of treating a tissue region inside a body containing cancer cells comprising contacting said target tissue with a device according to claims 1-66; and coupling energy into said device from an external magnetic field so as to heat the magnetic materials which thereby heat the surrounding tumor bed tissue. 71. A method of treating a cancerous tissue inside a body comprising: removing a portion of the cancerous tissue thereby leaving a resection cavity in the body, inserting into said resection cavity a device of claims 1-66, using at least one additional piece of pre-gelled polymer to provide additional spacing between the polymer core and the surrounding tumor resection cavity wall; injecting viscous liquid polymer around the core implant that will solidify to hold the thermobrachytherapy delivering device in appropriate position centered within the resection cavity. 72. The method of claim 71, further comprising the step of coupling energy into said device with an external magnetic field so as to heat up said device. 73. A method of treating a cancerous tissue in a body comprising: removing a portion of the cancerous mass, thereby leaving a resection cavity in the body, inserting into said resection cavity a device according to claims 60-66; and filling said hollow central space with an expandable material so as to expand and fill the resection cavity with said thermobrachytherapy device; and coupling energy into said device with an external magnetic field so as to heat up said device. 74. A method of treating a cancerous tissue in a body comprising: removing a portion of the cancerous mass, thereby leaving a resection cavity in the body, inserting into said resection cavity a device of claims 45-59 and filling said hollow central core with an expandable material so as to expand and fill the resection cavity with said device; and coupling energy into said device with an external magnetic field so as to heat up said device and thereby improve the local delivery of chemotherapeutics and sensitize surrounding tumor bed tissue to radiation, drugs, and immune stimulating treatments over an extended period of time. 75. A method of treating a tissue region inside a body containing cancer cells comprising contacting said target tissue with a multimodality implant device, wherein said device comprises a polymer core, chemotherapy and or immunotherapy agents, and magnetic materials of Claims 1-44; and coupling energy into said device from an external magnetic field so as to heat the ferromagnetic materials which thereby heat the surrounding tumor bed tissue. 76. A method of treating a tissue region inside a body containing cancer cells comprising contacting said target tissue with a multimodality implant device, wherein said device comprises a polymer core, chemotherapy and or immunotherapy agents, and magnetic materials of Claims 1-66; and coupling energy into said device from an external magnetic field so as to heat the ferromagnetic 

 materials which thereby heat the surrounding tumor bed tissue, and radiating tissue in the vicinity of the implant device with external beam radiation, either before, during, or after release of therapeutics from the polymer core.
